narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 59% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students' academic paths, family backgrounds, and bar outcomes, focusing on whether someone is likely to pass right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower part of your class compared to others who typically succeed on the bar. This stood out as the most influential concern, as stronger end-of-undergrad rankings often correlate with bar success in the model's view of historical trends. Next, your LSAT score of 27—much lower than the average 36.7—pushed the prediction further toward failure, highlighting a gap in the standardized reasoning skills that have proven key for many bar passers. On a brighter note, your above-average undergrad GPA worked strongly in your favor, countering some of the risk by showing consistent academic strength earlier in your studies. Your middle family income bracket and full-time student status aligned with the norm for most applicants, so they didn't sway the decision much either way. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having some strengths like GPA, but the weaker Year 3 performance and LSAT created a riskier picture than the averages suggest for bar success. While this rejection stings, especially given your background and dedication to full-time study, it doesn't define your potential—many in similar situations have improved through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, perhaps while strengthening late-stage academic habits through additional coursework or prep programs tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. Admissions teams often re-evaluate strengthened applications, and building these areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically in future cycles. You're already ahead with that GPA; channeling focus here positions you well for another shot.
